techniques.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
techniques.evidence_quote: attackers poisoned the NPM package “bignum” by hijacking the S3 bucket serving binaries
whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file

attack_chain.description: Attacker noticed an abandoned/deleted S3 bucket that had previously hosted binaries for the npm package 'bignum' and re-registered (hijacked) the bucket under the same globally-unique name.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.

evidence_chunks.quote: an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.
an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.

attack_chain.description: Attacker uploaded a malicious .node binary to the hijacked S3 bucket, mimicking the original binary expected by the bignum package.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This counterfeit .node binary mimicked the functions of the original file.

attack_chain.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain
attack_chain.description: Because vulnerable versions (0.12.2-0.13.0) of bignum continued to reference the old S3 URL via node-gyp during install, users installing the package fetched the malicious binary from the attacker-controlled bucket.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file, placed by the attacker

evidence_chunks.quote: whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file, placed by the attacker
whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file, placed by the attacker

attack_chain.description: Developer installation of the bignum package triggered download and loading of the malicious .node binary, executing the attacker's code on the victim's machine.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file

evidence_chunks.quote: users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file
users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file

attack_chain.description: The malicious binary harvested local machine environment variables and hostname from the victim system.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: steal the user id’s, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name

evidence_chunks.quote: steal the user id’s, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name
steal the user id’s, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name

attack_chain.description: The binary used getpwd and getuid to gather the current user identity information.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the binary file harvested data via functions like getpwd and getuid

evidence_chunks.quote: the binary file harvested data via functions like getpwd and getuid
the binary file harvested data via functions like getpwd and getuid

attack_chain.description: The binary opened a TCP/IPv4 socket and issued an HTTP GET request, hiding the stolen data inside the User-Agent header.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: created a TCP socket for IPv4 communication and covertly sent the collected data as a user-agent of a ‘GET’ request

evidence_chunks.quote: created a TCP socket for IPv4 communication and covertly sent the collected data as a user-agent of a ‘GET’ request
created a TCP socket for IPv4 communication and covertly sent the collected data as a user-agent of a ‘GET’ request

attack_chain.description: Stolen credentials and environment data were exfiltrated to the attacker's hijacked S3 bucket via the crafted GET request.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: exfiltrates the stolen data to the hijacked bucket

evidence_chunks.quote: exfiltrates the stolen data to the hijacked bucket
exfiltrates the stolen data to the hijacked bucket

impact_type: Credential Theft / Software Supply Chain Compromise
initial_access_vector: Hijacking of an abandoned AWS S3 bucket referenced by the npm package 'bignum' (via node-gyp at install time); the attacker re-registered the globally unique bucket name after the original was deleted and replaced the legitimate install-time binary with a malicious .node binary.
persistence_mechanism: Ongoing control of the hijacked S3 bucket name, which continued to be fetched by any user installing vulnerable versions (0.12.2-0.13.0) of bignum.

nodes.description: Attacker noticed an abandoned/deleted S3 bucket that had previously hosted binaries for the npm package 'bignum' and re-registered (hijacked) the bucket under the same globally-unique name.
nodes.evidence_quote: an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.

nodes.evidence_quote: This counterfeit .node binary mimicked the functions of the original file.

nodes.evidence_quote: users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file

nodes.description: The malicious binary harvested local machine environment variables and hostname from the victim system.
nodes.evidence_quote: steal the user id’s, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name

nodes.description: The binary opened a TCP/IPv4 socket and issued an HTTP GET request, hiding the stolen data inside the User-Agent header.
